metric_value: Turbulent; Oracle/Informix/Sybase revenues stalled or slumped
notes: 1997 and early 1998: high-profile revenue struggles across leading vendors; questions about DBMS production and sales model

metric_value: Moderate pace overall; Unix enterprise flat; newer segments exploding
notes: Aberdeen: DBMS market will continue growing but fundamentally altered; segmentation accelerating; Unix-server segment slowest; NT/BI/mobile fastest

metric_value: <$200 million
notes: 'Heralded a decade ago as next defining technology'; small but vibrant segment; assimilated mainly into ORDBMSs

metric_name: ORDBMS share of new DBMS revenue
metric_value: >50% of new DBMS revenue (first year ORDBMS > pure RDBMS)
notes: Oracle8 launch 1997 drove this; most deploying ORDBMSs don't yet leverage object capabilities; Aberdeen: object capabilities increasingly important

metric_value: ~$2 billion 1998 license revenues
notes: De facto standard DBMS on Unix servers; leads market; Oracle service revenues as large as software licensing; comprehensive platform coverage

metric_value: Will continue to dominate Unix enterprise but face commoditization pressure
notes: Aberdeen: Oracle must alter direct sales strategy to support VARs/ISVs; active in almost every new segment; Oracle8 well-positioned for Web multi-tier

metric_value: >50% annual revenue growth
notes: Among largest DBMSs in unit sales; bundled with Microsoft Back Office; aggressive pricing; majority Windows NT DBMS market share

metric_value: May be the biggest DBMS story of 1998
notes: Due H2 1998; could cement NT DBMS lead and extend into enterprise and business intelligence; adds row-level locking, scalability, data warehousing

metric_value: Revenue restatement and lowering in 1997; refocused strategy
notes: Four target segments: high-performance OLTP, data warehousing/BI, Web/content management, distributed enterprise; excellent DataBlade technology; pushing ISV/ERP partnerships

metric_value: Can capture high-end OLTP/BI/ERP but will struggle in commoditized NT segments
notes: Aberdeen: will struggle where market becomes commoditized; innovative DataBlade and excellent performance = healthy chunk of high-end; Web content/dev segment obstacle

metric_value: Suffering from marketplace dynamics despite good product; mobile and tools products driving fortunes
notes: Strategy: distinguish DBMS by leveraging more successful tools/middleware/mobile products; tough time building enterprise DBMS market share

metric_value: Will expand overall share particularly on IBM servers; will emerge as BI and e-commerce leader
notes: Aberdeen: will struggle to beat Oracle/Microsoft in mainstream packaged-application-driven Unix and NT markets; strong business intelligence and e-commerce positioning

metric_value: First enterprise ODBMS from major vendor; off to good start but ahead of mass market
notes: CA can't yet bank on Jasmine as major revenue source; 1998 critical year for Ingres/DBMS given increased competition

metric_value: Explosive growth continuing; branch offices/departments/SMB scaling NT
notes: Buyers looking for 'turnkey' and 'almost-lights-out' solutions; looking especially for aggressively-priced commoditized DBMSs

metric_value: NT should scale sufficiently for many enterprise production apps within 2-3 years
notes: Aberdeen: NT Clustering will fundamentally alter enterprise production market segment; Microsoft SQL Server will be enterprise-ready further extending volume model

metric_value: Windows NT growth, packaged applications model, mass-deployment sales
notes: Per-unit and per-seat revenues dropping in key segments; overall DBMS expenditures will still increase despite lower per-unit costs; consulting/services booming

metric_value: Consulting/services business outpacing software licensing revenues
notes: Oracle service revenues about equal to software licensing revenue; Informix and Sybase greatly expanding service operations; shortage of capable programmers

metric_name: Packaged application influence on DBMS buying
metric_value: Packaged applications (SAP/Baan/PeopleSoft/Oracle) increasingly drive DBMS purchasing
notes: Enterprises buying packaged applications instead of custom-developing; DBMS seen as embedded engine; fundamentally changes revenue and channel models

metric_name: Object-Relational vs. pure Object DBMS split
metric_value: IBM/Oracle/Informix advocate ORDBMS; Microsoft/CA/Sybase advocate separate relational+object databases
notes: Three-way: ORDBMS advocates (IBM, Oracle, Informix) vs. separate databases advocates (Microsoft, CA, Sybase); Aberdeen believes object capabilities increasingly important

metric_value: Java support has become a required DBMS capability
notes: Internet technologies tremendously expand data access; Java support for new Internet applications; stored procedures and triggers written in Java

metric_name: Business intelligence / data warehousing DBMS segment
metric_value: Very strong unit sales and revenues; one of hottest DBMS sub-segments
notes: High-growth and high-profit in 1997; all major DBMS vendors targeting this segment; requires powerful query optimizers and parallel-scalable capabilities

metric_name: RDBMS + ORDBMS combined new revenue share
metric_value: >80% of all new DBMS revenues
notes: RDBMSs and ORDBMSs dominate minicomputer/PC LAN/mid-range server markets; gradually gaining in mainframe and PC sectors

metric_value: Market subdividing into: enterprise production / enterprise DSS / branch office / SMB / single PC / thin client / mobile / PCD
notes: Figure 5: Primary DBMS Market Segments; each segment with distinct technical and business values, distribution channels, users, purchasers

metric_value: Aberdeen does NOT expect near-term consolidation among major vendors; expects major vendors to buy small start-ups
notes: Major vendors will buy small start-ups to more quickly enter and occupy newer market segments; competitive dynamics remain between Oracle/IBM/Microsoft/Informix/Sybase/CA

metric_value: Significant new niche market; very strong unit sales growth
notes: Fueled by enterprise decentralization and laptop/PCD success; new applications: SFA and field service programs; market viability depends entirely on application success

metric_value: All major DBMS suppliers must reposition in 1998 for new segments; VAR/ISV relationships are defining success factor
notes: Aberdeen anticipates: launch products for BI/mobile/SMB segments; beef up services; launch second-gen Web middleware; VAR relationships most important success factor

metric_value: Must alter traditional aggressive direct sales strategy to support VARs/ISVs and packaged application vendors
notes: Oracle's challenge: traditional direct sales model doesn't match new DBMS marketplace; packaged application vendors now primary channel

metric_value: Will lead in niche segments including component-based app development; remain minor player in mainstream
notes: Aberdeen: pure object DBMSs will begin to lead in several niche market segments but remain minor in mainstream; ORDBMS will dominate general-purpose

metric_value: Vast majority of IBM DBMS sales (DB2/VSAM/IMS) are for mainframe systems
notes: IBM: mainframe heritage; DB2 Universal Database gives foothold on Unix and NT; leverages hardware bundling and VAR relationships

metric_value: RDBMSs have been dominant database model for new installations for past decade
notes: Structured data in tabular sets; SQL query language; excellent distributed architecture adaptability; success driven by organizations' growing need to analyze information
